title: No chaos required: traversable wormhole signals survive 98% coupling deletion
abstract: The traversable wormhole protocol in coupled Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) systems produces a transmission signal C(t) widely interpreted as evidence of holographic dynamics. Recent work has questioned this interpretation, showing that similar signals arise in generic thermalizing systems. We address what the signal actually probes by systematically destroying quantum chaos in the SYK model via random coupling deletion, while monitoring the transmission signal across the chaos-to-integrable transition. Using exact diagonalization of the doubled SYK model at N=10 with 50 disorder realizations per sparsity, supplemented by Krylov-subspace extensions to N=20, we find that the ensemble-averaged peak height varies by less than 1.1% across a 50-fold sparsification range, even as the underlying spectrum transitions from Gaussian-unitary-ensemble to sub-Poisson statistics. A 1,200-instance sweep over the inter-system coupling mu confirms that the signal is controlled by mu alone, with no dependence on internal chaos. We further verify that the thermofield double state retains its thermal structure under sparsification despite substantial changes to the state vector, providing a structural explanation for the invariance. These results indicate that the transmission signal diagnoses inter-system coupling fidelity rather than holographic dynamics, and that future quantum-simulation experiments require independent chaos diagnostics to substantiate gravitational claims. As a practical consequence, the invariance implies that 98% of the Hamiltonian's coupling terms can be discarded (with variance rescaling of the survivors), reducing the gate count per Trotter step by approximately 50x at N=10 and bringing larger traversable-wormhole simulations within experimental reach.
summary: The paper demonstrates that the transmission signal in traversable wormhole simulations is independent of the system's internal quantum chaos. This decoupling means the signal primarily tracks inter-system coupling, allowing for highly efficient quantum simulations that discard up to 98% of Hamiltonian terms.
main_problem: Investigating whether the transmission signal in traversable wormhole protocols is a signature of holographic dynamics or merely a generic feature of inter-system coupling, and determining if this signal depends on internal quantum chaos.
main_result: The transmission signal is decoupled from internal chaos, remaining invariant even when 98% of the Hamiltonian couplings are deleted, which implies that the signal probes inter-system coupling fidelity rather than gravity-like dynamics.
method: Numerical simulations using exact diagonalization (ED) for small systems and Krylov-subspace extensions (Lanczos) for larger systems, combined with level spacing analysis and Lindblad master equation evolution.
model_or_system: A doubled Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model consisting of two sides (Left and Right) in a Thermofield Double (TFD) state, connected by a weak Maldacova-Qi bilinear interaction.
key_observables: Transmission signal peak height |C(t*)|, peak time t*, Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM), level spacing ratio <r> (chaos diagnostic), and entanglement entropy.
important_parameters: Sparsity parameter p (fraction of surviving couplings), inter-system coupling strength mu, inverse temperature beta, and dephasing rate gamma.
main_assumptions: The study is restricted to the q=4 SYK model and relies on numerical results without a complete analytical proof.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the transition from GUE to sub-Poisson statistics via sparsity and the corresponding invariance of the transmission peak height; other figures illustrate TFD structural diagnostics and noise sensitivity.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of signal interpretation, describes the sparsification methodology, presents results on signal-chaos decoupling, analyzes the robustness of the TFD state, and concludes with implications for quantum simulation complexity.
why_interesting: It provides critical insight into the validity of using quantum circuits to simulate holographic gravity and offers a massive reduction in gate complexity for simulating wormhole-like protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Signal-chaos decoupling at N = 10, β = 8, µ = 0.1 (50 realizations per point). (a) Level spacing ratio ⟨r⟩ver- sus sparsity p for N = 10 (filled circles) and N = 14 (open squares). Dashed lines indicate GUE (⟨r⟩= 0.603) and Pois- son (⟨r⟩= 0.386) reference values. (b) Transmission peak height |C(t∗)| versus p. Error bars denote SEM. The dashed line marks the dense (p = 1.0) mean. The signal varies by less than 1.1% despite a complete loss of quantum chaos.

Figure 1(b) shows the main result: the transmission peak height |C(t∗)| as a function of sparsity, computed from 50 disorder realizations at each of nine sparsity values from p = 1.0 to p = 0.02. The peak height is |C(t∗)| = 0.924 ± 0.001 at p = 1.0 and 0.915 ± 0.009

FIG. 2. Transmission peak height versus inter-system cou- pling µ for three sparsity levels: p = 1.0 (chaotic, circles), p = 0.1 (edge of chaos, squares), and p = 0.05 (non-chaotic, triangles). N = 10, β = 8, 50 realizations. Error bars denote SEM. The three curves overlap at each µ value, confirming that the signal is controlled by the coupling, not by the inter- nal dynamics.

FIG. 3. Noise robustness of the transmission signal at N = 8, β = 8, µ = 0.1 (30 realizations). (a) Peak height |C(t∗)| versus dephasing rate γ for four sparsity levels. The curves overlap, confirming that noise sensitivity is independent of sparsity. The dashed line marks the 50% degradation thresh- old. (b) Critical dephasing rate γ∗(at which the signal drops to 50% of its noiseless value) versus sparsity. γ∗is constant at ≈0.055 J.

FIG. 4. Distribution of peak heights across 50 disorder real- izations at each sparsity (violin plot with jittered individual data points). The mean (horizontal markers inside each vi- olin) is approximately constant across all sparsities, but the spread grows from σ = 0.006 at p = 1.0 to σ = 0.061 at p = 0.02. Dashed line: dense mean.
